title: Dark state spectroscopy in nonlinear waveguide quantum electrodynamics
authors: Shay Nadel, Amir Sivan, Aviv Karnieli
abstract: Quantum systems face a fundamental trade-off: they must remain decoupled from the environment to maintain long coherence times, yet they require interactions with the environment to be accessible for measurement. As a prime example, emitter arrays coupled to waveguides facilitate collective modes that, owing to interference, can suppress radiation into the waveguide. While complete destructive interference creates perfectly dark states with infinite lifetimes, their inherent decoupling makes them unmeasurable in standard waveguide quantum electrodynamics. Consequently, current approaches must rely on system non-idealities that permit measurement but limit the coherence times. In this work, we lift this limitation by proposing the use of weakly squeezed light generated in \{chi}(2) nonlinear waveguides for the spectroscopy of completely dark states. We show that the fluorescence spectrum probes transitions between the dressed dark states of the emitter array. This work paves the way towards the measurement and control of dark states, with applications for robust quantum memories, computation, and communication.
summary: The paper proposes using weakly squeezed light generated by nonlinear waveguides to spectroscopically measure 'dark states' in emitter arrays coupled to photonic waveguides. By analyzing the output photon spectrum, the authors demonstrate that otherwise unmeasurable collective excitations can be probed. This technique offers a pathway for robust quantum state characterization crucial for quantum memory and computation.
main_problem: The fundamental challenge of measuring quantum states that are perfectly decoupled from the environment (dark states) while maintaining long coherence times.
main_result: Weakly squeezed light generated in nonlinear waveguides can enable spectroscopy, allowing the measurement and characterization of these otherwise inaccessible dark states.
method: The analysis uses the Lindblad master equation formalism combined with input-output theory to calculate the incoherent power spectrum $S(\omega)$ from output photon correlations.
model_or_system: The system consists of an array of emitter qubits coupled to a one-dimensional photonic waveguide, enhanced by $\chi^{(2)}$ nonlinearity realized in superconducting circuits. The dynamics are governed by an effective interaction Hamiltonian and associated jump operators.
key_observables: Incoherent power spectrum $S(\omega)$ measured via the Fourier transform of output photon correlations; spectral peaks corresponding to dark-manifold transition frequencies.
important_parameters: Weak squeezing parameter ($r$), coupling efficiency ($ eta$), number of emitters ($N=4, 6$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on Born-Markov and rotating-wave approximations, assuming the system is dominated by dark states in steady state for small squeezing.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the power spectrum $S(\omega)$ showing distinct peaks matching calculated transition frequencies for weak squeezing. Later figures demonstrate that these spectral signatures persist even when accounting for parasitic decay ($\gamma_0$) and reduced coupling efficiency ($ eta$).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the theoretical framework by modeling the WQED interaction, then develops the methodology using master equations to calculate $S(\omega)$. It presents results showing how squeezing enables measurement, followed by an analysis quantifying the robustness of this technique against realistic nonidealities like decay.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses open quantum systems dynamics in cavity QED settings. The use of nonlinear parametric amplification (squeezing) to probe dark states is a sophisticated extension of standard linear coupling models, providing new tools for quantum memory and computation readout.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. System illustration. A waveguide with a nonlin- ear section of length L (red cylinder), pumped from the left and the right with coherent light at frequency 2ω (violet). The χ(2) nonlinearity introduces a parametric gain, generat- ing two photons in frequency ω and wavelength λ within the nonlinear segment. Identical two-level emitters with a tran- sition energy of ℏω are spaced by a wavelength and coupled to the nonlinear segment (depicted is the case of 4 emitters in blue). The output signal is collected from the right side of the waveguide. The emitter array can radiate with rate γ into the waveguide modes, and with rate γ0 to free-space. Within the nonlinear section, owing to the...

FIG. 2. Dark-state manifold and steady-state spec- troscopy for an ideal system (γ0 = 0) and N = 4 emit- ters. (a) Classification of the collective modes within the total angular momentum basis |j, m⟩. The dark states |Di⟩, which are the |j, −j⟩states, are color-coded depending on the value of their j-parity. (b) Dark eigenstates |ψi⟩of the Hamil- tonian Hint (Eq. (6a)) projected onto the dark-state manifold are illustrated with respect to their eigenvalue relative to the emitter transition. Only transitions between different j-parity values are allowed by the jump operators (Eq. (6b)). The allowed transitions are depicted as curly lines, with two dis- tinct colors matching ∆E1 = 0, ∆E2 =...

FIG. 3. Power spectrum for N = 6 emitters across varying squeezing parameters in the ideal case (γ0 = 0). (a) Heatmap of the incoherent steady-state power spectrum in logarithmic scale (log10), normalized for each value of r independently. The solid black lines correspond to allowed transition frequencies between dark eigenstates of the projected Hamiltonian PHintP. They show excellent agreement with the power spectrum peaks for small squeezing parameter r. For increasing values of r, the spectral features are broadened and spaced apart, making the peaks less distinct. (b), (c) Spectral line cross sections corresponding to the horizontal red lines in panel (a) for r = 0.026 and r = 0.015,...

Fig. 4 illustrates the steady-state incoherent power spectrum of the right-moving waveguide mode for an ar- ray of N = 4 emitters under varying coupling efficiencies β. The ideal case (β = 1) exhibits sharp, symmetric peaks positioned exactly at the allowed transition fre- quencies. However, as the coupling to free space increases (β = 0.999 and β = 0.995), two distinct spectral changes emerge. First, the resonance peaks undergo significant broadening. This is an expected consequence of the free- space decay introducing an additional decoherence chan- nel, which fundamentally decreases the lifetime of the dark states. Second, as highlighted in the inset of Fig. 4,

FIG. 4. Incoherent power spectrum for a nonideal system. Incoherent power spectrum at steady-state in loga- rithmic scale (log10) are plotted for different values of waveg- uide coupling efficiency β = γ/ (γ + γ0). β = 1 refers to the ideal case where γ0 = 0. The plots are calculated with squeez- ing parameter r = 0.01 and waveguide decay rate γ = 1.0. Inset is a zoom-in around the peaks of the curves. It shows broadening and Fano-like shifts in the peak position relative to the transition frequencies.

Partially Pumped Collective Emitters.note: The new paper describes spectroscopy in 'emitter qubits' coupled to a waveguide, which directly relates to the concept of collective emission and characterization within an array of emitters, analogous to studying local drive or selective excitation.
